card.title: Fitts's Law: Why Big Buttons Win
card.teaser: A 1954 paper about arm movement quietly decides how every app on your phone gets laid out.
card.coverImagePrompt: A close-up of a fingertip hovering just above a glowing circular button on a dark interface, subtle motion blur implying a swift approach, cinematic product photography

lessons.title: Why the Mac dock is at the edge
lessons.body: Move your mouse to the bottom of the screen as fast as you can. Did you aim carefully? Probably not — you slammed it downward, knowing the edge would stop it. Apple's dock sits there on purpose. The edge of the screen is effectively infinitely wide, which makes edge buttons faster to hit than any button in the middle. This is Fitts's law in action.

visual.imagePrompt: A cursor flying toward the bottom edge of a macOS desktop where the dock waits, long motion-blur streak behind it

lessons.title: The equation in plain English
lessons.body: In 1954, psychologist Paul Fitts measured how long it takes a human to move a hand to a target. He found a simple pattern: time grows with distance and shrinks with target size, on a logarithmic curve. In plain English — the farther away or smaller a button is, the longer you take to hit it, and small buttons hurt far more than small distances.

keyTerms.definition: The width of the button along the direction of movement
A Fitts-law measure combining distance and size into one number

visual.imagePrompt: A chalkboard diagram of a hand moving toward a target with the Fitts's law equation written beside it, academic aesthetic

lessons.title: The magic of the edge
lessons.body: The most famous consequence of Fitts's law is the 'infinite edge.' When a target sits against the screen edge, you can overshoot as much as you want and the cursor still lands on it. Effectively, its size is infinite. Corners are even better — they're targets of infinite size in two directions. That's why macOS puts the Apple menu in the top-left corner, and why the taskbar in Windows sits along a full edge.

visual.imagePrompt: A highlighted top-left corner of a screen glowing with an overlaid Fitts's-law diagram showing infinite target area

lessons.title: Touchscreens and fat fingers
lessons.body: Apple's Human Interface Guidelines recommend touch targets of at least 44×44 points; Google recommends 48dp. Those numbers weren't plucked from the air — they come from repeated Fitts-law studies showing error rates explode for targets smaller than the fingertip's roughly 10mm contact patch. Shrink a button below that and your users don't become more skilled; they just start missing.
lessons.funFact: In the original 1954 study, Fitts had participants tap between metal plates with a stylus — the same simple setup reappeared decades later in iPhone user-testing labs.

visual.imagePrompt: A hand tapping a phone screen with a transparent overlay showing a 44-point touch-target ring, clean editorial style

lessons.title: Where does your most-used button live?
lessons.body: Look at the interface you're designing. Find the single action users do most. If it's small, or far from where the pointer starts, or floating in the middle of the screen, you've already made users slower. Fitts's law is the cheapest usability fix in design — and it's 70 years old.

quiz.question: According to Fitts's law, which two properties determine how long it takes to hit a target?
options: Distance to target and target size
The user's age and skill level
quiz.explanation: Fitts's law models movement time as a logarithmic function of distance divided by target size.

quiz.question: Why are the edges and corners of a screen excellent places for buttons?
options: The cursor stops there, making them effectively infinite in size
They are closer to where users start moving
They require less processing to render
quiz.explanation: Overshoots don't matter at an edge — the cursor stops — so the target is effectively unlimited in that direction.

quiz.question: What's Apple's recommended minimum touch-target size in iOS?
quiz.explanation: Apple's Human Interface Guidelines specify a minimum of 44×44 points for reliable fingertip targeting.

sources.title: The information capacity of the human motor system in controlling the amplitude of movement
sources.publisher: Journal of Experimental Psychology (Paul M. Fitts)
